title: Local-First Knowledge Bases
hook: Priya Nair on why a folder of markdown files outlives every SaaS knowledge tool — and why agents make plain text the power move.
takeaways: Notes that can't survive the company that made the editor were never your notes — a deprecated export feature turns them into hostages.
A personal knowledge base has one user and a fifty-year horizon; no vendor roadmap survives that, but a folder of text files already has.
Plain text lets a language model be a full citizen of the wiki — reading and writing every page through the filesystem, no API keys required.
SaaS AI sidebars see one document at a time; a local agent with grep sees the whole graph.
Honest costs of local-first: mobile is genuinely worse and links rot on careless renames — weekend problems, versus the decade problem of lock-in.

quotes.text: So my rule now is simple: if my notes can't survive the company that made the editor, they were never my notes.
Markdown is boring in the way load-bearing things should be boring.

people.name: Priya Nair
people.note: Built cloud knowledge tools for a decade, now an advocate of plain-markdown, local-first personal wikis

topics.name: Personal Knowledge Bases
topics.why: The core argument: one user, fifty-year horizon, so durability beats features.

topics.name: Local-First Software
topics.why: Files on disk versus SaaS hostage-taking; weekend problems versus decade problems.

topics.name: Markdown Wikis
topics.why: Plain markdown as the deliberately boring, load-bearing format for a lifetime of notes.
